analogy: Think of "Executive" through the chapter's own opening images: 78 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Four EXECUTIVE INTRODUCTION Legislature, executive and judiciary are the three organs of government. Together, they perform the functions of the government, maintain law and order and look after the welfare of the people. The Constitution ensures that they work... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: EXECUTIVE, INTRODUCTION, WHAT IS AN EXECUTIVE?, WHAT ARE THE DIFFERENT TYPES OF EXECUTIVE?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Executive is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 78 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Four EXECUTIVE INTRODUCTION Legislature, executive and judiciary are the three organs of government.

The text explains that together, they perform the functions of the government, maintain law and order and look after the welfare of the people.

The text explains that the Constitution ensures that they work in coordination with each other and maintain a balance among themselves.

The text explains that in a parliamentary system, executive and the legislature are interdependent: the legislature controls the executive, and, in turn, is controlled by the executive.

The text explains that in this chapter we shall discuss the composition, structure and function of the executive organ of the government.

The text explains that this chapter will also tell you about the changes that have occurred in recent times due to political practice.

The text explains that after reading this chapter, you will be able to ± make a distinction between the parliamentary and the presidential executive; ± understand the constitutional position of the President of India; ± know the composition and functioning of the Council of Ministers and the importance of the Prime Minister; and ± understand the importance and functioning of the administrative machinery.

The text explains that 79 Chapter 4: Executive WHAT IS AN EXECUTIVE?

The text explains that who is in charge of the administration of your school?

The text explains that who takes important decisions in a school or a university?

The text explains that in any organisation, some office holder has to take decisions and implement those decisions.

The text explains that we call this activity administration or management.

The text explains that but administration requires a body at the top that will take policy decisions or the big decisions and supervise and coordinate the routine administrative functioning.

The text explains that you may have heard about the executives of big companies, banks or industrial units.

The text explains that every formal group has a body of those who function as the chief administrators or the executives of that organisation.

The text explains that some office holders decide the policies and rules and regulations and then some office holders implement those decisions in actual day-to-day functioning of the organisation.

The text explains that in the case of government also, one body may take policy decisions and decide about rules and regulations, while the other one would be in charge of implementing those rules.

The text explains that the organ of government that primarily looks after the function of implementation and administration is called the executive.

The text explains that executive is the branch of government responsible for the implementation of laws and policies adopted by the legislature.

The text explains that while the heads of government and their ministers, saddled with the overall responsibility of government policy, are together known as the political executive, those responsible for day to day administration are called the permanent executive.

The text explains that 80 Indian Constitution at Work WHAT ARE THE DIFFERENT TYPES OF EXECUTIVE?

The text explains that to answer this question we will briefly outline the nature of executive existing in some of these countries.

The text explains that the USA has a presidential system and executive powers are in the hands of the president.

The text explains that canada has a parliamentary democracy with a constitutional monarchy where Queen Elizabeth II is the formal chief of state and the prime minister is the head of government.

The text explains that in France, both the president and the prime minister are a part of the semi- presidential system.

The text explains that japan has a parliamentary system with the Emperor as the head of the state and the prime minister as the head of government.

The text explains that italy has a parliamentary system with the president as the formal head of state and the prime minister as the head of government.

The text explains that russia has a semi-presidential system where president is the head of state and prime minister, who is appointed by the president, is the head of government.

The text explains that germany has a parliamentary system in which president is the ceremonial head of state and the chancellor is the head of government.

The text explains that in a presidential system, the president is the Head of state as well as head of government.

The text explains that in this system the office of president is very powerful, both in theory and practice.

The text explains that countries with such a system include the United States, Brazil and most nations in Latin America.

The text explains that 82 Indian Constitution at Work Semi-Presidential Executive in Sri Lanka In 1978 the constitution of Sri Lanka was amended and the system of Executive Presidency was introduced.

The text explains that under the system of Executive Presidency, people directly elect the President.

The text explains that the President has vast powers under the constitution.

The text explains that apart from being the elected Head of State and the Commander-in- Chief of the Armed Forces, the President is also the Head of the Government.

The text explains that in a parliamentary system, the prime minister is the head of government.

The text explains that most parliamentary systems have a president or a monarch who is the nominal Head of state.

The text explains that in such a system, the role of president or monarch is primarily ceremonial and prime minister along with the cabinet wields effective power.

The text explains that countries with such system include Germany, Italy, Japan, United Kingdom as well as Portugal.

The text explains that a semi-presidential system has both a president and a prime minister but unlike the parliamentary system the president may possess significant day-to-day powers.

The text explains that in this system, it is possible that sometimes the president and the prime minister may belong to the same party and at times they may belong to two different parties and thus, would be opposed to each other.

The text explains that countries with such a system include France, Russia, Sri Lanka, etc.

The text explains that 83 Chapter 4: Executive P P P P PA AA AARLI RLI RLI RLI RLIA AA AAM M M M ME EE E EN N N N NTTTTTA AA AA RY RY RY RY RY E E E E EXEC XEC XEC XEC XECUTI UTI UTI UTI UTIVE VE VE VE VE IIIIIN N N N N IIIIIN N N N ND D D D DIIIIIA AA AA When the Constitution of India was written, India already had some experience of running the parliamentary system under the Acts of 1919 and 1935.

The text explains that but after all, the Prime Minister has a greater say in policy making and in choosing the ministers.

The text explains that why do you think is the advice of the Council of Ministers binding on the President?

The text explains that the parliamentary system of executive vests many powers in the legislature for controlling the executive.

The text explains that why, do you think, is it so necessary to control the executive?

The text explains that it is said that there is too much political interference in the working of the administrative machinery.

The text explains that it is suggested that there should be more and more autonomous agencies which do not have to answer to the ministers. a.

The text explains that do you think this will make administration more people- friendly? b.

The text explains that do you think this will make administration more efficient? c.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 4: Executive
